                "event": "Flood Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Hastings NE",
                "headline": "Flood Advisory issued February 8 at 10:03AM CST until February 8 at 10:00PM CST by NWS Hastings NE",
                "description": "* WHAT...Flooding caused by an ice jam is expected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of central and south central Nebraska, including\nthe following counties, in central Nebraska, Merrick. In south\ncentral Nebraska, Hall and Hamilton.\n\n* WHEN...Until 1000 PM CST Sunday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Minor flooding in low-lying and poor drainage areas.\nOverflowing poor drainage areas.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 1000 AM CST, The public reported an ice jam near\nInterstate 80 just east of Highway 281 resulting in water\ncoming out of the Platte River and filling the ditches near\nthe Interstate.\n- Some locations that will experience flooding include...\nGrand Island, Phillips and Chapman.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Be aware of your surroundings and do not drive on flooded roads.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "FLSGID"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WGUS83 KGID 081603"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "FLOOD ADVISORY FOR AN ICE JAM IN EFFECT UNTIL 10 PM CST THIS EVENING"
                    ],

For the Low Water Advisory, abnormally\nlow water levels.\n\n* WHERE...Tidal Potomac from Indian Head to Cobb Island MD.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 6 PM EST this\nevening. For the Low Water Advisory, until 3 PM EST this\nafternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Conditions will be hazardous to small craft. Below\nnormal water levels will result in hazardous navigating\nconditions.",

                "headline": "High Wind Warning issued February 8 at 5:34AM HST until February 9 at 6:00PM HST by NWS Honolulu HI",
                "description": "* WHAT...Northeast winds 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 60 mph are\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...All Hawaiian Islands.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 PM HST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Damaging winds may blow down trees and power lines and\ndamage roofs.                 "instruction": "Strong winds may lead to property damage.                 "description": "* WHAT...Northeast winds 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 60 mph\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...All Hawaiian Islands.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 PM HST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Damaging winds may blow down trees and power lines and\ndamage roofs.                 "instruction": "Strong winds may lead to property damage.                 "headline": "Flood Advisory issued February 8 at 5:33AM HST until February 8 at 9:00AM HST by NWS Honolulu HI",
                "description": "* WHAT...Flooding caused by excessive rainfall continues.\n\n* WHERE...The island of Hawaii in Hawaii County.\n\n* WHEN...Until 900 AM HST.\n\n* IMPACTS...Minor flooding on roads, poor drainage areas, and in\nstreams.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 533 AM HST, radar indicated moderate to locally heavy rain\nover windward Big Island. Rain was falling at a rate of 1 to\n2 inches per hour in the heaviest showers.\n- Some locations that will experience flooding include...\nHilo, Hawaiian Paradise Park, Kapaau, Honokaa, Honomu,\nPapaikou, Pepeekeo, Hakalau, Ninole, Laupahoehoe, Mountain\nView, Glenwood, Volcano, Ookala, Keaau, Paauilo, Hawaiian\nAcres, Orchidlands Estates and Hawaii Volcanoes National\nPark.",
                "instruction": "Stay away from streams, drainage ditches and low lying areas prone\nto flooding.\n\nA Flood Watch is also in effect until 6 PM HST Monday.",

                "description": "* WHAT...Large breaking waves of 4 to 7 feet with dangerous rip\ncurrents.\n\n* WHERE...Malibu Coast and Los Angeles County Beaches.\n\n* WHEN...Until noon PST today.\n\n* IMPACTS...There is an increased risk for ocean drowning.                 "event": "High Surf Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Juan PR",
                "headline": "High Surf Advisory issued February 8 at 11:00AM AST until February 10 at 6:00PM AST by NWS San Juan PR",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the High Surf Advisory, large breaking waves of 10 ft.\nFor the HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK, life-threatening rip current.\n\n* WHERE... For the HIGH SURF ADVISORY, the most vulnerable areas\nfor high surf and beach erosion include Pinones along Road 187,\nParcelas Suarez, Villas del Mar, and Villa Christiana in Loiza;\nFortuna in Luquillo; Pueblo and Tamarindo in Aguadilla; Bario\nObrero, Pueblo and Jareaito in Arecibo; Las Boca in Barceloneta;\nMachuca in Manati; Puerto Nuevo in Vega Baja; Ocean Park in San\nJuan; and Barrio Espinal in Aguada. For the HIGH RIP CURRENT\nRISK St. Thomas and adjacent islands.\n\n* WHEN...For the High Rip Current Risk remains through wednesday\nafternoon. For the High Surf Advisory remains from 6 AM monday\nto 6 PM AST tuesday\n\n* IMPACTS...FOR THE HIGH SURF ADVISORY, High waves that can wash\nover jetties and sweep people and pets onto jagged rocks.\nRough surf may also knock you down. Large breaking waves will\nresult in localized beach/dune structure erosion and dangerous\nswimming conditions. FOR THE HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK, rip\ncurrents can sweep even the best swimmers away from shore into\ndeeper water, where it becomes difficult to return to safety.",
                "instruction": "There is a high risk of rip currents.\n\nRip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as groins, jetties\nand piers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nIf you become caught in a rip current, yell for help. Remain\ncalm, do not exhaust yourself and stay afloat while waiting for\nhelp. If you have to swim out of a rip current, swim parallel to\nshore and back toward the beach when possible. Do not attempt to\nswim directly against a rip current as you will tire quickly.\n\nA High Surf Advisory means that high surf will affect beaches in\nthe advisory area, producing localized beach erosion and\ndangerous swimming conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWSJU"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHCA42 TJSJ 081500"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK REMAINS IN EFFECT THROUGH WEDNESDAY AFTERNOON... ...HIGH SURF ADVISORY REMAINS IN EFFECT FROM 6 AM MONDAY TO 6 PM AST TUESDAY"
                    ],

                "event": "Rip Current Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Juan PR",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued February 8 at 11:00AM AST until February 11 at 6:00PM AST by NWS San Juan PR",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the High Surf Advisory, large breaking waves of 10 ft.\nFor the HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK, life-threatening rip current.\n\n* WHERE... For the HIGH SURF ADVISORY, the most vulnerable areas\nfor high surf and beach erosion include Pinones along Road 187,\nParcelas Suarez, Villas del Mar, and Villa Christiana in Loiza;\nFortuna in Luquillo; Pueblo and Tamarindo in Aguadilla; Bario\nObrero, Pueblo and Jareaito in Arecibo; Las Boca in Barceloneta;\nMachuca in Manati; Puerto Nuevo in Vega Baja; Ocean Park in San\nJuan; and Barrio Espinal in Aguada. For the HIGH RIP CURRENT\nRISK St. Thomas and adjacent islands.\n\n* WHEN...For the High Rip Current Risk remains through wednesday\nafternoon. For the High Surf Advisory remains from 6 AM monday\nto 6 PM AST tuesday\n\n* IMPACTS...FOR THE HIGH SURF ADVISORY, High waves that can wash\nover jetties and sweep people and pets onto jagged rocks.\nRough surf may also knock you down. Large breaking waves will\nresult in localized beach/dune structure erosion and dangerous\nswimming conditions. FOR THE HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK, rip\ncurrents can sweep even the best swimmers away from shore into\ndeeper water, where it becomes difficult to return to safety.",
                "instruction": "There is a high risk of rip currents.\n\nRip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as groins, jetties\nand piers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nIf you become caught in a rip current, yell for help. Remain\ncalm, do not exhaust yourself and stay afloat while waiting for\nhelp. If you have to swim out of a rip current, swim parallel to\nshore and back toward the beach when possible. Do not attempt to\nswim directly against a rip current as you will tire quickly.\n\nA High Surf Advisory means that high surf will affect beaches in\nthe advisory area, producing localized beach erosion and\ndangerous swimming conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "event": "Gale Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Mount Holly NJ",
                "headline": "Gale Warning issued February 8 at 9:58AM EST until February 8 at 12:00PM EST by NWS Mount Holly NJ",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 20 to 25 kt with\ngusts up to 35 kt and seas 5 to 7 ft. For the Heavy Freezing\nSpray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour\nor greater may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal waters from Sandy Hook to Manasquan Inlet NJ\nout 20 nm.\n\n* WHEN...Until noon EST today.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous seas which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {

                        "MWWPHI"

                "event": "Gale Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Mount Holly NJ",
                "headline": "Gale Warning issued February 8 at 9:58AM EST until February 8 at 12:00PM EST by NWS Mount Holly NJ",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds around 25 kt with\ngusts up to 35 kt and seas 5 to 7 ft. For the Heavy Freezing\nSpray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour\nor greater may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal waters from Manasquan Inlet to Little Egg\nInlet NJ out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Little Egg Inlet to\nGreat Egg Inlet NJ out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Great Egg\nInlet to Cape May NJ out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Cape May\nNJ to Cape Henlopen DE out 20 nm and Coastal waters from Cape\nHenlopen to Fenwick Island DE out 20 nm.\n\n* WHEN...Until noon EST today.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous seas which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "headline": "Gale Warning issued February 8 at 9:55AM EST until February 8 at 1:00PM EST by NWS Wakefield VA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 20 to 25 kt with\ngusts up to 35 kt and seas 6 to 8 ft. For the Freezing Spray\nAdvisory, moderate accumulation of freezing spray.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal waters from Fenwick Island DE to Chincoteague\nVA out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Chincoteague to Parramore\nIsland VA out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Parramore Island to\nCape Charles Light VA out 20 nm and Coastal Waters from Cape\nCharles Light to Virginia-North Carolina border out to 20 nm.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, until 1 PM EST this afternoon.\nFor the Freezing Spray Advisory, until 4 PM EST this afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in freezing spray is hazardous.\nFreezing spray may render mechanical and electronic components\ninoperative.                 "senderName": "NWS Wakefield VA",
                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Advisory issued February 8 at 9:55AM EST until February 8 at 4:00PM EST by NWS Wakefield VA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 20 to 25 kt with\ngusts up to 35 kt and seas 6 to 8 ft. For the Freezing Spray\nAdvisory, moderate accumulation of freezing spray.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal waters from Fenwick Island DE to Chincoteague\nVA out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Chincoteague to Parramore\nIsland VA out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Parramore Island to\nCape Charles Light VA out 20 nm and Coastal Waters from Cape\nCharles Light to Virginia-North Carolina border out to 20 nm.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, until 1 PM EST this afternoon.\nFor the Freezing Spray Advisory, until 4 PM EST this afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in freezing spray is hazardous.\nFreezing spray may render mechanical and electronic components\ninoperative. Ice accretion on decks and superstructures may\nresult in some loss of stability. Strong winds will cause\nhazardous seas which could capsize or damage vessels and\nreduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for ice accumulation on their vessel and\nconsider altering plans to avoid or mitigate these hazardous\nconditions.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",

                "headline": "High Wind Warning issued February 8 at 4:56AM AKST until February 10 at 12:00PM AKST by NWS Anchorage AK",
                "description": "* WHAT...Southeast to southwest winds 45 to 60 mph, with gusts up to\n75 to 85 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Central Aleutians, including Adak and Atka.\n\n* WHEN...Until noon AKST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...High winds may move loose debris, damage property, and\ncause power outages. Travel will be difficult.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The Warning now covers 2 storms, with the\nfirst storm moving through from this morning until this evening,\nand the second storm moving through from Monday morning until\nTuesday morning.\nExpect a long duration of strong winds, with the highest winds\noccurring this afternoon for the first storm, and from Monday\nnight through Tuesday morning for the second storm. While winds\ndecrease between storms from this evening through Monday morning,\nexpect gusts to drop no lower than 25-35 mph.",
                "instruction": "Secure loose objects that could be blown around or damaged by the\nwind. Prepare for the possibility of widespread and long power\noutages. Use caution if you must travel.",

                "senderName": "NWS Anchorage AK",
                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued February 8 at 4:48AM AKST until February 9 at 6:00PM AKST by NWS Anchorage AK",
                "description": "* WHAT...Snow. Snow accumulations between 8 and 18 inches. Highest\namounts along the Tok Cutoff from Chistochina to Mentasta Pass.\nWinds gusting as high as 30 mph along the Tok Cutoff.\n\n* WHERE...Central Copper Valley and Tok Cutoff.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 PM AKST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be difficult, especially through Mentasta\nPass and along the Tok Cutoff. The snow will be powdery given the\ncold temperatures. The combination of heavy snowfall, gusty winds,\nand easily transportable snow could result in significantly\nreduced visibilities on area roadways. Near whiteout conditions at\ntimes are possible.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A long duration snow will bring periods of\nlight to moderate snowfall across portions of the Central Copper\nValley north of Glennallen and along the Tok Cutoff to Mentasta\nPass. Light snow will increase in intensity Sunday with the\npossibility of a brief lull before intensity increases again\nMonday. Snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour are possible\nat times Monday morning and afternoon. Snow will begin to taper\noff Monday evening.",
                "instruction": "Slow down and use caution while traveling. For the latest road\nconditions call 5 1 1 or visit 511.alaska.gov.",

                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued February 8 at 4:48AM AKST until February 9 at 6:00PM AKST by NWS Anchorage AK",
                "description": "* WHAT...Snow. Snow accumulations between 8 and 18 inches. Highest\namounts near McCarthy.\n\n* WHERE...McCarthy and Southern Copper Valley.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 PM AKST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult, especially along the\nEdgerton Highway.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A long duration snow will bring periods of\nlight to moderate snowfall across portions of the Southern Copper\nValley south along the Edgerton Highway to McCarthy. Light snow\nwill increase in intensity Sunday with the possibility of a brief\nlull before intensity increases again Monday. Snowfall rates of\none inch per hour are possible at times Monday morning. Snow will\nbegin to taper off Monday evening.",
                "instruction": "Slow down and use caution while traveling. For the latest road\nconditions call 5 1 1 or visit 511.alaska.gov.",

                "senderName": "NWS Honolulu HI",
                "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 8 at 3:27AM HST until February 9 at 6:00PM HST by NWS Honolulu HI",
                "description": "...WINTER STORM WARNING IN EFFECT FOR THE BIG ISLAND SUMMITS ABOVE\n11000 FEET ELEVATION...\n\n.A disturbance aloft in combination with abundant moisture will\nbring periods of heavy snowfall to the Big Island summits of Mauna\nKea and Mauna Loa through Monday.\n\n* WHAT...Periods of heavy snow with storm total snow accumulations\nfrom 10 to 16 inches are expected. Winds could gust as high as 50\nmph.\n\n* WHERE...Big Island Summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa above 11,000\nfeet elevation.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 PM HST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult to impossible. Blowing\nsnow will significantly reduce visibility at times, with periods\nof zero visibility.",
                "instruction": "A Winter Storm Warning means significant amounts of snow, sleet, and\nice are expected or occurring. Strong winds are also possible. This\nwill make travel very hazardous or impossible. Any travel plans to\nthe summits should be postponed until the threat diminishes.",

                "event": "Flood Watch",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Honolulu HI",
                "headline": "Flood Watch issued February 8 at 3:18AM HST until February 9 at 6:00PM HST by NWS Honolulu HI",
                "description": "...FLOOD WATCH IN EFFECT FOR ALL HAWAIIAN ISLANDS THROUGH MONDAY\nAFTERNOON...\n\n* WHAT...Flash flooding caused by periods of excessive rainfall and\nterrain anchoring storms will continue.\n\n* WHERE...All Hawaiian islands.\n\n* WHEN...Through Monday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Flood prone roads and other low lying areas may be\nclosed due to elevated runoff and overflowing streams. Urban areas\nmay receive more significant flooding and property damage due to\nrapid runoff.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- A surface trough near the island of Maui continues to blanket\nthe Hawaiian Islands in deep unstable tropical moisture\nthrough Monday. An upper level low just west of the islands\nand windy trade winds will produce periods of heavy rain, a\nfew thunderstorms and flash flooding threats lasting through\nMonday afternoon. The highest threat for flash flooding will\nremain along the windward slopes of each island potentially\ndriven by terrain anchored heavy showers.",
                "instruction": "You should monitor later forecasts and be prepared to take action\nshould Flash Flood Warnings be issued.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "senderName": "NWS Upton NY",
                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning issued February 8 at 7:04AM EST until February 8 at 6:00PM EST by NWS Upton NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing\nspray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or greater may rapidly\naccumulate on vessels. For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest\nwinds 20 to 25 kt with gusts up to 30 kt and seas 3 to 5 feet\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...Long Island Sound.\n\n* WHEN...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, until 6 PM EST\nthis evening. For the Small Craft Advisory, until 10 PM EST\nthis evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Conditions will be hazardous to small craft.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nInexperienced mariners, especially those operating smaller\nvessels, should avoid navigating in hazardous conditions.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 20 to 25 kt with\ngusts up to 40 kt and seas 5 to 8 ft.                 "headline": "Fire Weather Watch issued February 8 at 2:09AM MST until February 9 at 5:00PM MST by NWS Denver CO",
                "description": "The National Weather Service in Denver has issued a Fire Weather\nWatch for wind and low relative humidity, which is in effect from\nMonday morning through Monday afternoon.\n\n* AFFECTED AREA...Fire Weather Zones 238, 242, 248, 250 and 251.\n\n* TIMING...From Monday morning through Monday afternoon.\n\n* WINDS...West 10 to 20 mph with gusts up to 35 mph.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as 14 percent.\n\n* IMPACTS...Conditions will be favorable for rapid fire spread.\nAvoid outdoor burning and any activity that may produce a\nspark and start a wildfire.",

                "event": "Flood Watch",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Omaha/Valley NE",
                "headline": "Flood Watch issued February 8 at 3:07AM CST until February 9 at 10:00PM CST by NWS Omaha/Valley NE",
                "description": "A watch remains in effect for the Platte, Elkhorn and Loup rivers\ndue to potential ice jam development. This heightened threat is due\nto persistent warm weather. Those with interests along area rivers,\nespecially in low-lying areas, should remain vigilant to the\npotential for rapidly rising water.\n\n* WHAT...Flooding caused by an ice jam continues to be possible.\n\n* WHERE...Low-lying areas along the Platte, Loup and Elkhorn rivers\nin Butler, Dodge, Douglas, Sarpy, Saunders, Washington, Antelope,\nColfax, Cuming, Madison, Pierce, Platte, Stanton and Cass counties.\n\n* WHEN...Through Monday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...The formation of an ice jam could result in sudden rises\nof water, leading to a rapid flooding situation. Ice jams can\ncause water level fluctuations of 1 to 3 feet over a very short\ntime period and without much notice.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- While no ice movement has been observed or reported, several\nconsecutive days of highs ranging from the upper 40s to mid\n60s suggest breakup and movement is imminent.",
                "instruction": "You should monitor later forecasts and be alert for possible Flood\nWarnings. Those living in areas prone to flooding should be prepared\nto take action should flooding develop.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "FFAOAX"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WGUS63 KOAX 080907"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [

                "headline": "Fire Weather Watch issued February 8 at 1:23AM MST until February 9 at 6:00PM MST by NWS Albuquerque NM",
                "description": "...FIRE WEATHER WATCH IN EFFECT LATE MONDAY MORNING THROUGH EARLY\nMONDAY EVENING ACROSS EAST CENTRAL NEW MEXICO DUE TO STRONG WINDS\nAND LOW HUMIDITY...\n\n.Stronger winds will develop during the day Monday with very dry\nair also moving in. This will increase the potential for critical\nfire weather along with a higher risk for rapid fire spread.\n\nThe National Weather Service in Albuquerque has issued a Fire\nWeather Watch...which is in effect from Monday morning through\nMonday afternoon.\n\n* AREA AND TIMING...This Watch includes much of northeastern and\neast central New Mexico (Fire Weather Zones 104, 123, 125, and\n126) with the greatest area of concern focused in San Miguel,\nnorthern Guadalupe, Quay, and Harding counties. The Watch is in\neffect from 11 AM MST through 6 PM MST Monday.\n\n* 20 FOOT WINDS...Sustained from the southwest at 20 to 25 mph\nwith occasional gusts to 35 mph.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...Falling to 7 to 13 percent in the late\nafternoon Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Any fires that develop will likely spread rapidly.\nOutdoor burning is not recommended.",
                "instruction": "Please advise the appropriate officials or fire crews in the\nfield of this Fire Weather Watch.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "RFWABQ"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWUS85 KABQ 080823"
                    ],

                "description": "* WHAT...Snow expected. Total snow accumulations of 6 to 12 inches,\nincluding over Togwotee and Teton Passes.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Extreme Cold Warning, dangerously cold wind chills\nas low as 10 below. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind\nchills as low as 5 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Caroline, Kent MD, Queen Annes, and Talbot Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Extreme Cold Warning, until noon EST today. For the\nCold Weather Advisory, from noon today to 10 AM EST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 10 below zero could\nresult in hypothermia if precautions are not taken.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nMonitor the latest forecasts and warnings for updates.\n\nIf you do not have heat in your home or lack shelter, you can call\n211 for assistance locating appropriate shelter from the cold.\n\nUse caution while traveling outside.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Extreme Cold Warning, dangerously cold wind chills\nas low as 21 below. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind\nchills as low as 14 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...In New Jersey, Eastern Bergen, Eastern Passaic, and\nWestern Bergen Counties. In New York, Northern Westchester,\nPutnam, Rockland, and Southern Westchester Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Extreme Cold Warning, until 1 PM EST this\nafternoon. For the Cold Weather Advisory, from 1 PM this afternoon\nto 10 AM EST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills could cause frostbite on exposed\nskin in as little as 30 minutes.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors.                 "headline": "Extreme Cold Warning issued February 8 at 1:32AM EST until February 8 at 1:00PM EST by NWS Burlington VT",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerously cold wind chills of 20 to 35 below zero\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...The St Lawrence Valley and Adirondacks in northern New\nYork.\n\n* WHEN...Until 1 PM EST this afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 35 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 10 minutes.\nFrostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is\nexposed to these temperatures.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The coldest period will be near sunrise this\nmorning. Additional cold headlines will likely be needed for\ntonight into Monday morning.",
                "instruction": "Persons are urged to stay indoors until conditions improve. If you\nmust go outside, dress in layers. Several layers of clothes will\nkeep you warmer than a single heavy coat. Cover exposed skin to\nreduce your risk of frostbite or hypothermia. Gloves, a scarf, and a\nhat will keep you from losing your body heat.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors.                 "description": "Dead brush and leaf litter have become very dry in northeast\nGeorgia, and will continue to dry today as relative humidity\nbecomes very low, falling to less than 25 percent.                 "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nTo prevent water pipes from freezing; wrap or drain or allow them to\ndrip slowly.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nUse caution while traveling outside.                 "headline": "Extreme Cold Warning issued February 8 at 12:53AM EST until February 8 at 1:00PM EST by NWS Albany NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Extreme Cold Warning, dangerously cold wind chills\nas low as 20 to 30 below. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold\nwind chills as low as 15 to 25 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Schoharie, Western Albany, Western Greene, Western\nSchenectady, Western Ulster, Montgomery, Northern Fulton, Southern\nFulton, and Southern Herkimer Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Extreme Cold Warning, until 1 PM EST this\nafternoon. For the Cold Weather Advisory, from 1 PM this afternoon\nto 10 AM EST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 30 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 15 to 30\nminutes. Frostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin\nis exposed to these temperatures.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nTo prevent water pipes from freezing; wrap or drain or allow them to\ndrip slowly.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nUse caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Extreme Cold Warning, dangerously cold wind chills\nas low as 28 below.                 "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Melbourne FL",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued February 8 at 12:34AM EST until February 9 at 4:00AM EST by NWS Melbourne FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerous rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal Volusia, Brevard, Indian River, Saint Lucie, and\nMartin Counties.\n\n* WHEN...Through late tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water.",
                "instruction": "Rip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as jetties and\npiers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nEntering the surf is strongly discouraged. If caught in a rip\ncurrent, relax and float. Don't swim against the current. If\nable, swim in a direction following the shoreline. If unable to\nescape, face the shore and call or wave for help.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWMLB"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHUS42 KMLB 080534"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [

                "event": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Boston/Norton MA",
                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning issued February 8 at 12:19AM EST until February 9 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Boston/Norton MA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 25 to 35 kt with\ngusts up to 40 kt and seas 4 to 7 feet. For the Heavy Freezing\nSpray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour\nor greater may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Cape Cod Bay, Nantucket Sound and Massachusetts Bay\nand Ipswich Bay.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, until 1 PM EST this afternoon.\nFor the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, until 7 AM EST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous seas which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation. During freezing spray conditions, the U.S.\nCoast Guard advises mariners to check and ensure all life-saving\ndevices remain free of ice.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "MWWBOX"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHUS71 KBOX 080519"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "GALE WARNING REMAINS IN EFFECT UNTIL 1 PM EST THIS AFTERNOON... ...HEAVY FREEZING SPRAY WARNING REMAINS IN EFFECT UNTIL 7 AM EST MONDAY"
                    ],
                    "BLOCKCHANNEL": [
                        "EAS",

                        "CMAS"
                    ],

                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Advisory issued February 8 at 12:19AM EST until February 8 at 7:00PM EST by NWS Boston/Norton MA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 20 to 30 kt with\ngusts up to 35 kt and waves around 2 feet. For the Freezing\nSpray Advisory, moderate accumulation of freezing spray.\n\n* WHERE...Boston Harbor.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, until 7 AM EST this morning. For\nthe Freezing Spray Advisory, until 7 PM EST this evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in freezing spray is hazardous.\nFreezing spray may render mechanical and electronic components\ninoperative. Ice accretion on decks and superstructures may\nresult in some loss of stability. Strong winds will cause\nhazardous waves which could capsize or damage vessels and\nreduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for ice accumulation on their vessel and\nconsider altering plans to avoid or mitigate these hazardous\nconditions.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, north winds 25 to 35 kt with\ngusts up to 40 kt and seas 7 to 11 feet.                 "event": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Boston/Norton MA",
                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning issued February 8 at 12:19AM EST until February 9 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Boston/Norton MA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, north winds 25 to 35 kt with\ngusts up to 40 kt and seas 7 to 11 feet. For the Heavy\nFreezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm\nper hour or greater may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal waters east of Ipswich Bay and the Stellwagen\nBank National Marine Sanctuary and Coastal waters from\nProvincetown MA to Chatham MA to Nantucket MA out 20 nm.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, until 1 PM EST this afternoon.\nFor the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, until 7 AM EST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous seas which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation. During freezing spray conditions, the U.S.\nCoast Guard advises mariners to check and ensure all life-saving\ndevices remain free of ice.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {

                    ],

                "senderName": "NWS Hastings NE",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued February 7 at 6:01PM CST by NWS Hastings NE",
                "description": "Continued well-above normal temperatures (especially through\nMonday) will cause increasing ice melt and ice movement along the\nPlatte, North Loup, Middle Loup, South Loup and Loup Rivers in\ncentral and south central Nebraska. This could result in the\npossible formation of localized ice jams. Although not a\ncertainty, should any ice jams happen to develop, water levels\ncould fluctuate as much as 1 to 3 feet in a very short time,\ncausing flooding with little, if any notice.\n\nIf any ice jam flooding is observed over the next few days along\nthe Platte or Loup River systems in central and south central\nNebraska, please report it immediately to the National Weather\nService in Hastings.",
